Photo by Markus Winkler on UnsplashFreelancing looked like freedom to me.
No fixed office. No strict boss. No unnecessary meetings. Just skills, internet, and income.
But before I understood how freelancing really works, I learned one painful lesson:
Not every client is real. And not every opportunity is safe.
This is the story of how I got scammed in my early career — and how that failure shaped me into a smarter developer.

When I Chose a Different Path
During college, most of my friends were cracking campus placements. I decided to wait. I wanted a job in my hometown.
I applied for a Web Developer internship through Naukri and got selected. The office was just 15 minutes from my home. I was excited.
But my excitement didn’t last long.
On the first day, reality hit.
The company operated from a small shopping complex building. The staircase was narrow. The entire office was a single room. There were only seven employees — including me.
And I was the only developer.
Everyone else was doing telemarketing.
They asked me to update their website and build new web applications. That part was fine. But the environment wasn’t.

• No proper workspace

• No separate lunch area (we ate at our desks)

• Calls intentionally scheduled at checkout time

• No clear working-hour boundaries
Within a week, it became clear that the role wasn’t aligned with the kind of career and work environment I was looking for.
I quit.
That decision pushed me toward freelancing.

My First Freelancing Project — And My First Mistake
I started exploring freelancing platforms. Since I had no strong portfolio, I began bidding low just to land my first project.
Eventually, I got one.
A simple single-page website.
My bid was $40.
It wasn’t about the money. It was about proving I could do it.
The client accepted my bid. I started working immediately.
During the project, the client asked me to connect on WhatsApp for “faster communication.” I didn’t think much about it. It felt normal.
That was my biggest mistake.
I completed the work and shared the project files.
The next day — I was blocked.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Lindsey LaMont on UnsplashOn WhatsApp.
On the freelancing platform.
Everywhere.
Just like that.
No payment.
No response.
No way to recover it.
I tried contacting the platform’s support team, but they couldn’t help. I had violated their policy by communicating outside the platform.
Technically, it was my fault.
But emotionally?
It felt like betrayal.

The Confidence Crash
That $40 loss hurt more than it should have.
It wasn’t the amount. It was what it represented.

• My first freelancing project

• My excitement

• My trust

• My confidence
For a few days, I genuinely thought:
Maybe freelancing isn’t for me.
But after the frustration settled, something changed.
Instead of quitting, I decided to learn.

What That Scam Taught Me
That experience gave me practical lessons no course ever could.
Here’s what I learned:
1. Never Move Communication Off the Platform
Freelancing platforms protect you only when you stay inside their ecosystem.
Once you move to WhatsApp, Telegram, or email — you lose protection.
2. Never Share Full Files Before Payment Milestones
Always use milestone payments.
Deliver partial previews if necessary, but never hand over everything before securing payment.
3. Check Client Profiles Carefully
Before accepting a project, review:

• Client rating

• Previous reviews

• Payment verification

• Hire rate
A blank or new profile with no history is most likely to be a red flag.
4. Avoid Clients Who Rush You to Move Fast
Scammers create urgency.
“Let’s do this quickly.”
“Send the files first.”
“We’ll settle payment later.”
Professional clients respect process.
5. Low Bids Attract Risk
When you bid extremely low, you sometimes attract people looking to exploit beginners.
Value your work — even if you’re just starting.

Why I’m Grateful for That Scam
It sounds strange, but I’m thankful it happened early.
If I had earned easy money without understanding risks, I might have made a bigger mistake later.
That $40 lesson saved me from future losses.
Freelancing isn’t a scam.
But freelancing without awareness is dangerous.

If you’ve faced something similar in freelancing, you’re not alone.
Every professional has at least one painful story behind their growth.
The key difference?
They didn’t quit.
Have you ever been scammed or faced a difficult experience in freelancing? I’d love to hear your story. Share your experience in the comments — your lesson might help someone avoid making the same mistake.
